title: 11 - Heart - Exam Prep Questions
description: Third-party generated single-choice exam-prep questions for Chapter 11. The set is aligned primarily to the Chapter 11 textbook content and shaped to reflect the style, tone, and choice distribution of the RCPA Basic Pathological Sciences mock exam format.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These questions are third-party generated study materials. disclaimer.answer_key_note: Answers are included as stable choice keys with brief source-grounded rationales for study and review purposes.

questions.question:
A 68-year-old man with long-standing systemic hypertension develops marked thickening of the left ventricular wall without marked chamber dilation.

What structural adaptation best explains this finding?

choices.text: Parallel addition of sarcomeres causing concentric hypertrophy
Series addition of sarcomeres causing eccentric hypertrophy
Diffuse myofiber disarray from a sarcomeric mutation
Loss of myocytes with compensatory fatty replacement
Deposition of amyloid within the ventricular wall

answer.reason: Pressure overload states such as systemic hypertension produce concentric hypertrophy by adding new sarcomeres in parallel.

questions.question:
A patient presents with dyspnea, orthopnea, bibasal crackles, and hemosiderin-laden macrophages in alveoli.

Which hemodynamic abnormality is most likely present?

choices.text: Elevated pulmonary venous pressure from left-sided heart failure
Isolated systemic venous congestion from right-sided heart failure
Obstruction of the superior vena cava
Pulmonary arterial hypertension without left-heart disease

answer.reason: Orthopnea, pulmonary edema, and heart failure cells reflect pulmonary venous congestion caused by left-sided heart failure.

questions.question:
A man with advanced diffuse interstitial lung disease develops right ventricular hypertrophy, hepatomegaly, and peripheral edema.

What term best describes this form of heart disease?

answer.reason: Pulmonary hypertensive heart disease due to primary lung or pulmonary vascular disease is termed cor pulmonale.

questions.question:
A 34-year-old woman is found to have right atrial and right ventricular dilation after a paradoxical embolic stroke. The defect was well tolerated for years and is now being diagnosed in adulthood.

Which lesion is most likely?

answer.reason: ASDs are often well tolerated for years, commonly present in adulthood, and can permit paradoxical embolization.

questions.question:
A patient with a long-standing ventricular septal defect develops progressive cyanosis, clubbing, and polycythemia after years of pulmonary hypertension.

What complication has occurred?

answer.reason: Pulmonary hypertension can reverse a long-standing left-to-right shunt, producing right-to-left flow with cyanosis and clubbing, which is Eisenmenger syndrome.

questions.question:
A cyanotic infant has a boot-shaped heart on imaging and marked right ventricular hypertrophy.

Which additional lesion is classically part of tetralogy of Fallot?

answer.reason: Tetralogy of Fallot consists of VSD, RV outflow obstruction, overriding aorta, and RV hypertrophy.

questions.question:
A neonate has transposition of the great arteries.

What associated abnormality is essential for survival before definitive repair?

choices.text: A communication that allows mixing between the systemic and pulmonary circulations
Complete closure of the ductus arteriosus

answer.reason: Transposition creates parallel circulations, so survival depends on a shunt such as a VSD, PDA, or foramen ovale that permits blood mixing.

questions.question:
A young adult has upper-extremity hypertension, diminished femoral pulses, lower-limb claudication, and rib notching on chest imaging.

Which congenital lesion best explains these findings?

answer.reason: Postductal coarctation produces upper-body hypertension, weak lower-extremity pulses, and collateral intercostal flow with rib notching.

questions.question: Stable exertional angina most commonly appears when a fixed atherosclerotic coronary lesion narrows the lumen by approximately what degree?

answer.reason: Coronary stenoses greater than 70% are considered critical and typically produce symptoms when demand rises during exertion.

questions.question: Most cases of unstable angina and acute myocardial infarction are triggered by what immediate event within a coronary artery?

choices.text: Abrupt plaque disruption with superimposed thrombosis
Gradual concentric intimal fibrosis without inflammation
Compression of the coronary ostium by pericardial fluid

answer.reason: Acute coronary syndromes usually reflect plaque erosion or rupture that exposes thrombogenic material and rapidly generates thrombus.

questions.question: During prolonged myocardial ischemia, which region is injured first?

answer.reason: The subendocardium is furthest from epicardial coronary supply and is subjected to the highest intramural pressure, so necrosis begins there.

questions.question:
A patient has recurrent episodes of chest pain at rest that respond promptly to nitroglycerin. Coronary vasospasm is suspected.

Which form of angina is most likely?

answer.reason: Prinzmetal angina is caused by coronary vasospasm, often occurs at rest, and responds rapidly to vasodilators.

questions.question:
An electrocardiogram shows ST-segment elevation and later development of pathologic Q waves after acute coronary occlusion.

What pathologic pattern is most likely present?

choices.text: Subendocardial infarction confined to the inner third of the wall
Transmural infarction caused by persistent epicardial vessel occlusion
Remote healed infarction with dense scarring

answer.reason: ST-elevation myocardial infarction corresponds to a transmural infarct produced by sustained epicardial coronary occlusion.

questions.question:
A patient undergoes urgent angioplasty for an acute myocardial infarction. Histology later shows intensely eosinophilic cross-bands in damaged myocytes within a hemorrhagic infarct.

What is the name of this morphologic change?

answer.reason: Reperfusion injury can produce hemorrhagic infarction with contraction band necrosis from calcium-driven hypercontracture of irreversibly injured myocytes.

questions.question: A patient presents 8 days after onset of chest pain. Which laboratory test is most likely to remain clearly elevated from the infarct?

answer.reason: Cardiac troponins remain elevated for 7 to 10 days after infarction, far longer than CK-MB.

questions.question: At what interval after a transmural myocardial infarction is ventricular free-wall rupture most likely to occur?

answer.reason: Rupture risk peaks 3 to 7 days after MI, when necrotic myocardium is soft and friable but mature scar is not yet present.

questions.question:
Five days after a large anterior myocardial infarction, a patient suddenly collapses with pulseless electrical activity. Pericardial tamponade is found.

Which lesion most likely caused the decompensation?

choices.text: Mural thrombus embolization to the lungs

answer.reason: Free-wall rupture is the most common myocardial rupture after MI and causes rapidly fatal hemopericardium with tamponade.

questions.question:
Several months after a large anteroseptal infarct, imaging shows a thinned bulging segment of scarred ventricular wall. The lesion is associated with heart failure, arrhythmias, and mural thrombus but seldom ruptures.

What is the best diagnosis?

answer.reason: A late postinfarct ventricular aneurysm is a thin fibrous scar that predisposes to thrombus, arrhythmia, and heart failure but rarely ruptures.

questions.question:
A man dies suddenly while walking to work. Autopsy shows severe coronary atherosclerosis but no fresh myocardial necrosis.

What is the most likely immediate mechanism of death?

choices.text: Fatal arrhythmia triggered by ischemic electrical instability
Massive septic embolization from infective endocarditis
Complete AV block from rheumatic carditis

answer.reason: Most sudden cardiac deaths in patients with CAD are caused by lethal arrhythmias arising from electrically unstable ischemic myocardium rather than a fresh infarct.

questions.question:
A heart from a patient with long-standing hypertension shows concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, enlarged hyperchromatic boxcar nuclei, and interstitial fibrosis.

What is the best clinicopathologic diagnosis?

answer.reason: Concentric LV hypertrophy with boxcar nuclei and fibrosis in a hypertensive patient defines systemic hypertensive heart disease.

questions.question:
An older adult with severe calcific aortic stenosis develops exertional chest pain despite no new coronary lesion.

What best explains the angina?

choices.text: Concentric LV hypertrophy has increased oxygen demand beyond what the capillary supply can meet
Acute immune-complex valvulitis has destroyed the cusps
Vegetations are embolizing into intramural vessels
Right-to-left shunting has reduced coronary oxygen content
Atrial myxoma is intermittently occluding the aortic orifice

answer.reason: In severe calcific aortic stenosis, chronic pressure overload causes concentric LV hypertrophy that raises oxygen demand beyond available perfusion.

questions.question:
A 27-year-old woman has a midsystolic click on auscultation. Echocardiography shows a redundant mitral leaflet bowing into the left atrium during systole.

What is the underlying lesion?

choices.text: Calcific degeneration of the mitral annulus with commissural fusion
Fibroelastic thickening of the tricuspid valve
Myxomatous degeneration causing mitral valve prolapse

answer.reason: Myxomatous mitral valve disease produces floppy redundant leaflets that prolapse during systole and create the classic midsystolic click.

questions.question: Which lesion is pathognomonic for acute rheumatic carditis?

answer.reason: Acute rheumatic fever produces Aschoff bodies composed of inflammatory cells, including activated macrophages called Anitschkow cells.

questions.question:
A patient with a previously damaged mitral valve develops a low-grade fever and positive blood cultures after a dental procedure.

Which organism is classically responsible for this type of subacute infective endocarditis?

answer.reason: Viridans streptococci from the oral flora classically cause subacute infective endocarditis on previously abnormal valves after transient bacteremia.

questions.question:
A cachectic patient with metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma is found to have small sterile vegetations on previously normal cardiac valves.

What is the best diagnosis?

answer.reason: NBTE produces small sterile bland vegetations, often on previously normal valves, in hypercoagulable or wasting states such as mucinous adenocarcinoma.

questions.question:
A 42-year-old patient has a flabby enlarged heart with four-chamber dilation, mural thrombi, and an ejection fraction of 20%.

Which primary myocardial disorder best fits this picture?

answer.reason: Dilated cardiomyopathy is the classic flabby four-chamber dilation phenotype with severe systolic dysfunction and mural thrombus risk.

questions.question:
A competitive athlete dies suddenly. Autopsy shows severe thinning of the right ventricular free wall with fibrofatty replacement of myocardium.

Mutations in which class of proteins are most strongly associated with this disorder?

answer.reason: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy is strongly associated with mutations in desmosomal proteins at the intercalated disk.

questions.question:
A teenager collapses during exercise. The heart is heavy, nondilated, and shows marked asymmetric septal hypertrophy with myofiber disarray.

What is the most likely underlying cause?

choices.text: Autosomal dominant mutation in a sarcomeric protein

answer.reason: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is usually caused by autosomal dominant sarcomeric gain-of-function mutations and features asymmetric septal hypertrophy with myofiber disarray.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of myocarditis in the United States?

answer.reason: In the United States, viral myocarditis is the most common form, especially from coxsackieviruses and other enteroviruses.

questions.question:
A patient develops a rapidly accumulating 250-mL hemopericardium after myocardial rupture.

Why can this relatively small volume be fatal?

choices.text: Acute pericardial pressure prevents cardiac chamber filling, causing tamponade
Blood instantly calcifies and fixes the pericardium in place
The fluid triggers diffuse coronary vasospasm
The hemopericardium immediately causes bacterial pericarditis
A small volume always indicates severe chronic constrictive pericarditis

answer.reason: When blood accumulates rapidly, even a few hundred milliliters can acutely compress the heart and cause fatal cardiac tamponade.
